title: It's right there in the name
content: Lake plus warehouse equals lakehouse. The idea is to keep the cheap, open, flexible storage of a data lake, while adding the reliability, structure, and query performance associated with warehouses. The goal is one system that reduces the need to choose between flexibility and trust.
next: Why not just keep running both systems side by side?

title: Two systems, endless friction
content: Running a lake and a warehouse separately usually means copying data back and forth between them. Copies drift out of sync, storage and compute costs increase, and teams often build separate workflows around different systems. A unified architecture reduces that friction at the source.
next: How can cheap storage act like a reliable warehouse?

title: A smart layer on cheap storage
content: In a lakehouse, all your data still lives in cheap cloud storage, in open file formats. The trick is a smart software layer on top that adds warehouse-grade reliability and structure. You keep the low cost and openness, and gain trust.
next: Who gets to use this single store of data?

title: One copy, many uses
content: Because everything lives in one place, the same data can serve business dashboards, machine learning, analysts, and data scientists alike. The old split between separate BI and data science storage layers becomes smaller. This improves consistency and reduces repeated copying.
next: What does that mean for trust in the numbers?

title: Finally, a single source of truth
content: When many teams read from the same governed data, reports are more likely to agree. This brings organizations closer to the long-promised single source of truth. In practice, governance and modeling still matter, but the architecture makes consistency much easier.
next: How do you keep one giant store of data organized?

title: Refining data in layers
content: The lakehouse is usually organized with the medallion architecture: data flows through quality layers named after medals. Each layer is cleaner and more refined than the last, so quality improves step by step.
next: What's in the first, rawest layer?

title: Bronze: raw and untouched
content: The bronze layer holds raw data exactly as it arrived, from any source. Nothing is cleaned yet. Keeping the original preserves full fidelity and means you can always reprocess it later if your needs change.
next: What happens once the raw data is cleaned up?

title: Silver: cleaned and trustworthy
content: In the silver layer, data is cleaned, validated, deduplicated, and joined together. This is detailed, trustworthy data — the reliable foundation that analysts and data scientists can build on with confidence.

title: Gold: ready for the business
content: The gold layer holds business-ready data: aggregated tables and key metrics shaped for dashboards, reports, and decisions. It's the polished output the rest of the company actually consumes.
next: Why split the work into these three layers at all?

title: One place to govern it all
content: A single store of data needs strong governance. Unity Catalog provides a central place to control access, track lineage, and help people discover the data they need across the lakehouse. Governance is what keeps one shared platform from turning into a larger version of the old data swamp problem.
next: So what's the missing piece that makes it reliable?

title: Cheap storage, but reliable how?
content: The lakehouse promises warehouse reliability on cheap lake storage. But plain files in cloud storage have no transactions and no guarantees. Something has to add that reliability — turning loose files into trustworthy tables.
